title: Engineering to improve the precision and flexibility of crispr editing

This log summarizes key insights from the webinar The Future of CRISPR, hosted by The Scientist in June 2025.
Watch the webinar
In this blog im going talk about Ben Kleinstivers contribution as it is the most interesting to me.

title: Engineering to improve the precision and flexibility of crispr editing
text:
This log summarizes key insights from the webinar The Future of CRISPR, hosted by The Scientist in June 2025.
Watch the webinar
In this blog im going talk about Ben Kleinstivers contribution as it is the most interesting to me.

title: Engineering to improve the precision and flexibility of crispr editing
text:
Background

Cas9 is bacteria’s adaptive defense against Bacteriophages and cleaves invading DNA strands
Cas9 can only bind and cut DNA at sites adjacent to a PAM sequence.
Wild-type enzymes are naturally occurring enzymes

title: Engineering to improve the precision and flexibility of crispr editing
text:
What they created https://pammla.streamlit.app/

PAMmlla is a ML tool that predicts which Cas9 variant is optimal for that sequence’s PAM recognition to minimize off target activation.
They trained PAMmla on a library of cas9 proteins with 6 altered proteins. Expressed them in bacteria and created a selective pressure that required a specific SpCas9 against 1 of 16 different PAMS
They then Sequenced the specific domains, And expressed its biological activity against each AA sequence.
This was the data that PAMmla was trained on.

title: Engineering to improve the precision and flexibility of crispr editing
text:
Significance

PAMla has enabled gene editing into previously inaccessible regions of the genome, and reduces trial-and-error in therapeutic development.

title: Engineering to improve the precision and flexibility of crispr editing
text:
Questions

I wonder if this technology could be used within oncology on multiple gene targets to design combinatorial editing strategies.

title: 100 Days of Code
text: This section is part journal, part learning log. I’m using the #100DaysOfCode challenge to build consistent coding habits and sharpen my skills. Each entry will either be a quick write-up of what I’ve coded that day, or a summary of something I’ve read or practiced all with a focus on clean, professional documentation. Whether it’s a new project, debugging insight, or notes from a tutorial, this log is about steady progress and reflection.

🎥 Watch the webinar on Vimeo
📊 Download the slide deck (PDF)

title: AI in Life Sciences: What’s Changing and What’s Holding Us Back
text:
This log summarizes key insights from the webinar Inside the Minds of Scientists: How AI is Changing Life Science Research, hosted by Bioinformatics in May 2025.
🎥 Watch the webinar on Vimeo
📊 Download the slide deck (PDF)

title: AI in Life Sciences: What’s Changing and What’s Holding Us Back
text:
🚀 Key Trends

AI is moving from discovery to development.
AI appears to be shifting from predictions to decisions — Y Combinator is investing heavily in companies fully run by AI agents.
Pharma is integrating AI across more stages of R&D (e.g., target ID, trial design, decision-making).

title: AI in Life Sciences: What’s Changing and What’s Holding Us Back
section: 🧱 What’s Holding AI Back in Biology?
text:
🧱 What’s Holding AI Back in Biology?

Current issues with Ai in biosciences:


Reproducibility issues — hard to trust models without transparent workflows.
Learning curve and training — lack of experience with AI was a significant pain point for many users.

💬 This resonated with me. I’ve already seen how messy biological data can be, which leads to a steep learning curve and difficulty managing AI outputs.

title: AI in Life Sciences: What’s Changing and What’s Holding Us Back
text:
📘 Changes for future adoption

Transparency is important for scientists to adopt and understand AI tools — they need to know how and what models were trained with.
Tools need to be built with their actual users in mind — typically, biologists.

title: Prostate Cancer DEG Analysis (GSE6919)
text:
In this analysis, I used bulk RNA sequencing (microarray data) from the public GEO dataset GSE6919 to investigate differences in gene expression between cancerous and healthy prostate tissues.
The aim was to identify differentially expressed genes (DEGs) that could point to disrupted biological processes in prostate cancer, especially with a view toward metabolic shifts or biomarker discovery.

title: Prostate Cancer DEG Analysis (GSE6919)
text:
In this analysis, I used bulk RNA sequencing (microarray data) from the public GEO dataset GSE6919 to investigate differences in gene expression between cancerous and healthy prostate tissues.
The aim was to identify differentially expressed genes (DEGs) that could point to disrupted biological processes in prostate cancer, especially with a view toward metabolic shifts or biomarker discovery.

title: Prostate Cancer DEG Analysis (GSE6919)
text:
🔧 Methods Overview
The analysis was performed in R using the following packages:

GEOquery — to load expression and metadata

limma — for linear modeling of microarray data

tidyverse — for data wrangling and exploration

clusterProfiler, org.Hs.eg.db — for GO enrichment analysis


Key steps:

Downloaded GSE6919 and extracted the expression matrix

Cleaned and labeled metadata (e.g. tumor vs. normal)

Fit a linear model using limma and contrasted tumor vs. normal

Extracted top upregulated and downregulated genes

Performed GO enrichment to interpret biological meaning

title: Prostate Cancer DEG Analysis (GSE6919)
text:
📊 Differential Expression Results

Volcano Plot X axis is change in expression and y value is significance.



Volcano plot showing DEGs in prostate cancer


This volcano plot shows the spread of differentially expressed genes between tumor and normal tissues. Genes with high log fold-change and low p-values are highlighted.
This version of the volcano plot contains unfiltered or duplicated gene symbols and extreme values resulted in alot of messy invalid data scewing graph scale and bad gene labels such as GAPDH.1 and ACTB.1.

title: Prostate Cancer DEG Analysis (GSE6919)
text:
🧾Lessons Learned for myself
Plots didn’t save → forgot ggsave(). Used ggsave(“figures/Volcanoplot.jpg”) and made sure the folder path was right.
Plots didn’t render in site → wrong relative path. Switched to log/figures/ and renamed files cleanly.
enrichplot::barplot() didn’t work → Just use barplot() without enrichplot:: prefix.
PNG wouldn’t load → renamed .png and re-saved properly. Hosted on Imgur as backup.
Had to clean probe → gene symbol mapping → mapIds() gave NAs. Filtered out before topTable / enrichment.
Saved a bunch of useful code in .Rhistory — rescued a lot from there when I thought it was lost.
